name: upgrade
author: dojo-community
context: Refresh package indexes, apply upgrades, clean caches, and explain kept-back packages on Debian and Ubuntu hosts
info: This skill covers the system-wide maintenance workflow of APT: refreshing indexes with `apt-get update`, applying conservative upgrades, using `dist-upgrade` when dependency changes are expected, and cleaning stale package archives afterward. It also explains why packages may be kept back, including Ubuntu phased updates and dependency changes, so agents do not mistake safe behavior for a failure. Use it for patching hosts, preparing base images, and assessing whether a change requires a wider system upgrade.

sections.title: Refresh metadata before upgrades
sections.body: `apt-get update` resynchronizes local package indexes from configured sources. Debian documents it as the step that should always happen before `upgrade` or `dist-upgrade`. Without it, upgrade decisions are based on stale metadata.

sections.title: Upgrade vs dist-upgrade
sections.body: `upgrade` never removes installed packages, so it is conservative. `dist-upgrade` can remove or install packages to satisfy dependency transitions. Use `upgrade` for routine patching and `dist-upgrade` only when changed dependencies are acceptable and reviewed.

sections.title: Cleaning package and index caches
sections.body: `autoremove` prunes unneeded automatic dependencies. `autoclean` removes stale cached package files. `clean` removes all downloaded package archives. `distclean` removes nearly all list files under `/var/lib/apt/lists/` and is useful when producing redistributable images.

sections.title: Held-back packages and phased updates
sections.body: Ubuntu documents that packages kept back during `apt upgrade` are often the result of phased updates and can usually be left alone. Security updates are not phased. Use `apt policy <package>` to distinguish phased rollout behavior from pinning, third-party repository conflicts, or dependency changes that require deeper review.

links.context: Explain candidate versions or phased updates before forcing changes
Repair a system if the solver reports broken dependencies during upgrade
Review persistent preferences when the wrong source wins
Audit third-party sources before running broad upgrades

related.note: Use apt-cache policy to explain why a package is held back or selected from a specific source

scripts.name: Refresh and upgrade system packages
scripts.description: Run update, upgrade, dist-upgrade, and cleanup actions with apt-get

APT_UPGRADE_ACTION.description: Upgrade action: update, preview, upgrade, dist-upgrade, autoremove, autoclean, clean, or distclean

APT_ASSUME_YES.description: Set to true to pass -y to apt-get where supported

APT_SKIP_UPDATE.description: Set to true to skip apt-get update before upgrade or dist-upgrade

APT_SIMULATE.description: Set to true to simulate state-changing actions with -s

APT_QUIET.description: Set to true to add -q for quieter logging

action.description: Upgrade or cleanup action to execute

skipUpdate.description: Skip index refresh before upgrade actions

simulate.description: Simulate the action without modifying package state

depends.reason: Inspection is useful when upgrades are held back or when source precedence is unclear

body:
# Refreshing and Upgrading Systems

Use this node when the task is wider than a single package. APT separates metadata refresh from package changes, so upgrade workflows almost always start with `apt-get update`.

## Standard upgrade flow

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get -y upgrade
```

`upgrade` installs newer versions of already installed packages but will not remove existing packages. On Ubuntu, this is the default host-patching path.

## Full dependency-changing upgrade

```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get -y dist-upgrade
```

`dist-upgrade` can add or remove packages to satisfy changed dependencies. Use it when a package transition, kernel update, or release preparation requires solver flexibility. Review the plan first with `-s` in automation-sensitive environments.

## Cleanup

```bash
sudo apt-get autoremove --purge
sudo apt-get autoclean
sudo apt-get clean
```

- `autoremove` removes automatically installed packages that are no longer needed.
- `autoclean` removes cached package files that can no longer be downloaded.
- `clean` removes all cached package files.
- `distclean` removes most index files under `/var/lib/apt/lists/` and is mainly useful when finalizing images.

## Kept-back packages

On Ubuntu, `apt upgrade` may report packages as kept back because of phased updates. This is often normal and temporary, not a repair condition. Use [[apt/search#policy]] to inspect the version table and phasing information before forcing a package-specific install.

```bash
apt policy libglapi-mesa
```

If a package is kept back for a reason other than phasing, check repository policy in [[apt/pinning]] or solver state in [[apt/repair]].
